1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field $k$ and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve $C$ over $k$ (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set$ of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	$C/k$ projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	$\Hilb_{C/k}$ and its open subscheme $\Div_{C/k}$ of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective $k$-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for $\Pic$ assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map $\Div_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} $\Pic_{C/k}$. The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58	
59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on $C \times_k T$ to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor $D \subset C \times_k T$ over $T$
64	(an $S$-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of $C \times_k T$ flat over $T$, hence a $T$-point of the Hilbert
66	functor $\Hilb_{C/k}(T)$, which is the special case $\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}$
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)$ on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$.
70	
71	\begin{lemma}[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
72	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}
73	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
74	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, thm:pullback_natural}
75	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel (the Abel
76	  % map), and Thm.~th:repDiv (representability of relative effective
77	  % divisors by an open subscheme of $\IHilb_{X/S}$); arXiv:math/0504020,
78	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
79	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).
80	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
81	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
82	  %   Assume $X/S$ is projective and flat.  Then $\Div_{X/S}$ is
83	  %  representable by an open subscheme $\IDiv_{X/S}$ of the Hilbert scheme
84	  %  $\IHilb_{X/S}$.
85	  %   \end{thm}"
86	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
87	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
88	  %   The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
89	  %      $$A_{X/S}(T)\:\Div_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)$$
90	  %   defined by sending a relative effective divisor $D$ on $X_T/T$ to the
91	  %  sheaf $\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)$.  The target $\Pic_{X/S}$ may be replaced by any
92	  %  of its associated sheaves."
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3,
94	  Thm.~th:repDiv + Def.~dfn:Abel (cf.\ [Nitsure], ``Construction of Hilbert
95	  and Quot Schemes'', \S 1, where $\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}$).}
96	  Let $C/k$ be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve, and let
97	  $T$ be a $k$-scheme. There is a natural map of presheaves on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$
98	  \[
99	    A_{C/k} \;\colon\; \Div_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et},
100	    \qquad D \;\longmapsto\; [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)],
101	  \]
102	  where $\Div_{C/k}(T)$ is the set of relative effective Cartier divisors
103	  on $C \times_k T$ flat over $T$, and the target is the
104	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of
105	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The functor $\Div_{C/k}$ is representable
106	  by an open subscheme $\Div_{C/k} \subseteq \Hilb_{C/k}$ of the Hilbert scheme
107	  of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (with $\Hilb_{C/k}$ the special case
108	  $\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}$ of the Quot functor). The Abel map is therefore
109	  representable by a morphism of $k$-schemes $A_{C/k} : \Div_{C/k} \to
110	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
111	\end{lemma}
112	
113	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
114	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)
115	% "Set $H:=\IHilb_{X/S}$, and let $W\subset X\x H$ be the universal
116	% (closed) subscheme, and $q\:W\to H$ the projection.  Let $V$ denote the
117	% set of points $w\in W$ at which $W$ is an effective divisor. Plainly $V$
118	% is open in $W$.  Set $Z:=q(W-V)$.  Then $Z$ is closed because $q$ is
119	% proper.  Set $U:=H-Z$.  Then $U$ is open, and $q^{-1}U$ is an effective
120	% divisor in $X\x U$.  In fact, since $q$ is flat, $q^{-1}U$ is a relative
121	% effective divisor in $X\x U/U$.
122	% [\dots]"
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, thm:pullback_natural}
125	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
126	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}$ is representable by a $k$-scheme; this uses
127	  $C/k$ projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
128	  stronger). Let $W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}$ be the universal
129	  closed subscheme and $q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}$ the projection. The locus
130	  $V \subseteq W$ where $W$ is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
131	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement $W \setminus V$
132	  is closed in $W$, and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
133	  \Hilb_{C/k}$ is closed because $q$ is proper. Hence $\Div_{C/k} :=
134	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z$ is open in $\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
135	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on $\Div_{C/k}$ the
136	  restriction of $W$ is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
137	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
138	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
139	  \Div_{C/k} / \Div_{C/k}$.
140	
141	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor $D \subseteq C \times_k T$
142	  to the invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}$
143	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
144	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in $T$: pullback of an ideal
145	  sheaf along $\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T$
146	  takes the ideal of $D$ to the ideal of $D_{T'}$ (since $D$ is flat over
147	  $T$, the schematic inverse image $D_{T'}$ is a relative effective
148	  divisor on $C \times_k T'/T'$, again by Kleiman~\S 3 Lem.~lm:ctn), hence
149	  takes $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)$ to $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})$.
150	  Composing with the canonical projection
151	  $\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ of
152	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} and then with the
153	  \'etale sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} yields the
154	  natural map $A_{C/k}(T) : \Div_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$.
155	\end{proof}
156	
157	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
158	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
159	
160	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
161	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
162	of Route~A: $S = \Spec k$, $X = C$ a smooth proper geometrically integral
163	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4 Thm.~th:main) covers any
164	$X/S$ projective Zariski-locally over $S$, flat, with integral geometric
165	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
166	
167	\begin{theorem}[FGA representability of the Picard scheme]
168	  \label{thm:fga_pic_representability}
169	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
170	  \uses{def:pic_scheme, lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence,
171	        thm:quot_representable, def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification,
172	        thm:pullback_natural}
173	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main,
174	  % and Cor.~cor:algsch (specialisation to $S = \Spec k$, $X$ complete);
175	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~26, 35 (read from
176	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2155-L2166 + L2686-L2690).
177	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
178	  % "\begin{thm}[Main]\label{th:main}
179	  %   Assume $f\:X\to S$ is projective Zariski locally over $S$, and is flat
180	  %  with integral geometric fibers.
181	  %
182	  %  \tu{(1)} Then $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists, is separated and locally of finite
183	  %  type over $S$, and represents $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$.
184	  %
185	  %  \tu{(2)} If also $S$ is Noetherian and $X/S$ is projective, then
186	  %  $\IPic_{X/S}$ is a disjoint union of open
187	  %   subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective
188	  %  $S$-schemes.
189	  %   \end{thm}"
190	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
191	  % "\begin{sbscor}\label{cor:algsch}
192	  %    Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field $k$, and $X$ is complete.  Then
193	  %  $\IPic_{X/k}$ exists, represents $\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}$, and is a disjoint
194	  %  union of open quasi-projective subschemes.
195	  %   \end{sbscor}"
196	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main
197	  + Cor.~cor:algsch.}
198	  Let $k$ be a field and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
199	  over $k$. Then the \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor
200	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} is
201	  representable by a $k$-scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$, separated and locally of
202	  finite type over $k$, which is a disjoint union of open
203	  quasi-projective $k$-subschemes, each an increasing union of open
204	  quasi-projective subschemes. The scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$ is called the
205	  \emph{Picard scheme} of $C/k$.
206	\end{theorem}
207	
208	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
209	% L2168-L2366, proof of th:main; long and is broken into the four-step
210	% sketch below per the project's structural restatement convention. The
211	% verbatim quote of the opening reduction step is:)
212	% "By~\cite[(0, 4.5.5), p.~106]{EGAG}, it is a local matter on $S$ to
213	% represent a Zariski sheaf on the category of $S$-schemes.  Moreover, it
214	% is also a local matter on $S$ to prove that an $S$-scheme is separated
215	% and locally of finite type.  Hence, in order to prove (1), we may assume
216	% $S$ is Noetherian and $X/S$ is projective.
217	% [\dots]
218	% Plainly an $S$-scheme is separated if it is a disjoint union of
219	% separated open subschemes, or if it is an increasing union of
220	% separated open subschemes.  Hence (1) follows from (2)."
221	% (subsequent steps quoted inline in the project-notation proof below).
222	\begin{proof}
223	  \uses{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence, thm:quot_representable,
224	        def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification, thm:pullback_natural,
225	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
226	  We follow Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:main, specialised to $S = \Spec k$ and
227	  $X = C$. The proof proceeds in four conceptual steps; the underlying
228	  algebraic engine is the Quot scheme of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
229	  and the \'etale-sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
230	  together with the line-bundle / Quot correspondence of
231	  \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
232	
233	  \emph{Step 1: stratify by Hilbert polynomial.}
234	  For each polynomial $\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]$, let $P^\phi \subseteq
235	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ be the \'etale subsheaf whose $T$-points are
236	  represented by invertible sheaves $\mathcal{L}$ on $C \times_k T$ with
237	  $\chi(C_t, \mathcal{L}_t^{-1}(n)) = \phi(n)$ for all $t \in T$ (the
238	  Hilbert polynomial relative to the chosen polarisation
239	  $\mathcal{O}_C(1)$ on the projective curve $C$). The $P^\phi$ form a
240	  disjoint open cover of $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (openness via Kleiman's
241	  flat-base-change argument; cf.\ EGA III 7.9.11). It suffices to
242	  represent each $P^\phi$ by an increasing union of open quasi-projective
243	  $k$-schemes.
244	
245	  \emph{Step 2: bound twists by $m$-regularity.}
246	  Given $\phi$, fix $m$ large enough that the $T$-points of $P^\phi$ are
247	  representable by $\mathcal{L}$ on $C \times_k T$ satisfying
248	  $R^i \pi_{T*} \mathcal{L}(n) = 0$ for all $i \ge 1$, $n \ge m$. Twisting
249	  by $\mathcal{O}_C(m)$ defines an automorphism of $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$
250	  that carries $P^\phi$ to $P^{\phi_0}_0$ for $\phi_0(n) := \phi(m + n)$.
251	  Hence it suffices to represent each $P^{\phi_0}_0$ by a quasi-projective
252	  $k$-scheme.
253	
254	  \emph{Step 3: factor through the Abel map.}
255	  Consider the Abel map $A_{C/k} : \Div_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$
256	  of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}. The source $\Div_{C/k}$
257	  is representable by an open subscheme of $\Hilb_{C/k}$ (and hence by an
258	  open subscheme of the Quot scheme of \cref{thm:quot_representable}), and
259	  is quasi-projective over $k$. Pulling back along
260	  $P^{\phi_0}_0 \hookrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ yields an open
261	  subscheme $Z := P^{\phi_0}_0 \times_{\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}} \Div_{C/k}$
262	  of $\Div_{C/k}$, which is again quasi-projective. The projection
263	  $\alpha : Z \to P^{\phi_0}_0$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves:
264	  given a $T$-point $\lambda$ of $P^{\phi_0}_0$ represented by an \'etale
265	  cover $T' \to T$ and an invertible sheaf $\mathcal{L}'$ on $C \times_k
266	  T'$ with $H^1(C_{t'}, \mathcal{L}'_{t'}) = 0$, the projective bundle
267	  $\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to T'$ associated to
268	  $\mathcal{Q} := \pi_{T'*}\mathcal{L}'$ is smooth over $T'$; an \'etale
269	  cover $T_1 \to T'$ admits a $T'$-map $T_1 \to \mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q})$
270	  (EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii)) whose composition with the universal divisor map
271	  $\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to Z$ produces the required $T_1$-point of $Z$.
272	  Moreover, the first projection $Z \times_{P^{\phi_0}_0} Z \to Z$ is
273	  smooth and proper.
274	
275	  \emph{Step 4: descend by the smooth-proper quotient lemma.}
276	  Apply Kleiman~\S 4 Lem.~lm:qt: given a surjection $\alpha : Z \to P$
277	  of \'etale sheaves with $Z$ quasi-projective, $R := Z \times_P Z$
278	  representable, and the first projection $R \to Z$ smooth and proper,
279	  $P$ is representable by a quasi-projective scheme. In our case
280	  $P = P^{\phi_0}_0$, $Z$ and $R$ as above, so $P^{\phi_0}_0$ is
281	  quasi-projective. Reassembling over $\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]$ and
282	  over $m$, the disjoint union of representable $P^\phi$ pieces represents
283	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$. The total scheme is separated (as a disjoint
284	  union of separated quasi-projectives) and locally of finite type. This
285	  is Kleiman~\S 4 Cor.~cor:algsch in the $S = \Spec k$,
286	  $X = C$ complete case.
287	\end{proof}
288	
289	\subsection{The smooth-proper quotient lemma}
290	\label{subsec:fga_pic_qt}
291	
292	The structural lemma underlying step 4 above is recorded separately
293	because it is consumed verbatim in the assembly proof: it is the
294	abstract content allowing the descent from $Z = \Div^{\phi_0}_0$ to the
295	representing Picard piece.
296	
297	\begin{lemma}[Smooth-proper quotient of \'etale sheaves]
298	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}
299	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
300	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt;
301	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~31 (read from
302	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2368-L2375).
303	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
304	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lm:qt}
305	  %   Let $\alpha\:Z\to P$ be a map of \'etale sheaves, and set $R:=Z\x_PZ$.
306	  %  Assume $\alpha$ is a surjection, $Z$ is representable by a
307	  %  quasi-projective $S$-scheme, $R$ is representable by an $S$-scheme, and
308	  %  the first projection $R\to Z$ is representable by a smooth and proper
309	  %  map.  Then $P$ is representable by a quasi-projective $S$-scheme, and
310	  %  $\alpha$ is representable by a smooth map."
311	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt.}
312	  Let $\alpha : Z \to P$ be a morphism of \'etale sheaves on $(\Sch/k)$.
313	  Set $R := Z \times_P Z$. Suppose:
314	  \begin{enumerate}
315	    \item $\alpha$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves;
316	    \item $Z$ is representable by a quasi-projective $k$-scheme;
317	    \item $R$ is representable by a $k$-scheme;
318	    \item the first projection $R \to Z$ is representable by a smooth
319	      and proper map.
320	  \end{enumerate}
321	  Then $P$ is representable by a quasi-projective $k$-scheme, and $\alpha$
322	  is representable by a smooth map.
323	\end{lemma}
324

350	% is (represents) a surjection of \'etale sheaves.
351	% [\dots]
352	% Since $Z\to Q$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, $Q$ is, in the
353	% category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps
354	% $R\rightrightarrows Z$ by Exercise~\ref{ex:epi} below, which is an
355	% elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same
356	% exercise, $P$ too is the coequalizer of this pair of maps.  But, in any
357	% category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus $Q$
358	% represents $P$."
359	\begin{proof}
360	  By (ii) the structure map $Z \to \Spec k$ is quasi-projective, hence
361	  separated, so the first projection $Z \times_k Z \to Z$ is separated.
362	  By (iv) the first projection $R \to Z$ is proper; it factors through
363	  $h : R \to Z \times_k Z$ over the diagonal embedding (the second
364	  component is the second projection of $R \to Z \times_P Z$). The map
365	  $h$ is therefore proper, and a monomorphism (because $R$ is the graph
366	  of an equivalence relation on $Z$, hence injective on $T$-points).
367	  By EGA IV 8.11.5, a proper monomorphism is a closed immersion: $h$ is a
368	  closed embedding.
369	
370	  Hence $R \hookrightarrow Z \times_k Z$ is a flat (by (iv)) and proper
371	  equivalence relation on the quasi-projective $k$-scheme $Z$. By the
372	  theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
373	  quasi-projective schemes (Altman--Kleiman; the construction places
374	  the graph $R \subseteq Z \times_k Z$ inside the universal subscheme of
375	  the Hilbert scheme $\Hilb_{Z/k}$ and descends to a closed subscheme
376	  $Q \subseteq \Hilb_{Z/k}$ representing the quotient), there exist a
377	  quasi-projective $k$-scheme $Q$ and a faithfully flat, projective map
378	  $Z \to Q$ with $R = Z \times_Q Z$.
379	
380	  The map $Z \to Q$ is flat and proper; its fibers, up to field
381	  extension, coincide with those of $R \to Z$, which are smooth by
382	  hypothesis. Hence $Z \to Q$ is smooth.
383	
384	  Finally, $Z \to Q$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves (an \'etale
385	  cover of any $T$-point of $Q$ pulls back to a $T'$-point of $Z$, by
386	  EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii) applied to $Z \times_Q T \to T$). In the category
387	  of \'etale sheaves, both $Q$ and $P$ are coequalisers of the pair
388	  $R \rightrightarrows Z$; the coequaliser is unique up to unique
389	  isomorphism, so $Q$ represents $P$. Smoothness of $\alpha : Z \to P$
390	  is the smoothness of $Z \to Q$ just shown.
391	\end{proof}
392	
393	\section{Group-scheme structure}
394	\label{sec:fga_pic_group_structure}
395	
396	The Picard scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$ inherits an abelian group structure from
397	the tensor product of invertible sheaves on $C \times_k T$. The relative
398	Picard presheaf $T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T$ is already an abelian
399	group at every $T$ (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
400	functorially in $T$; this lifts to the \'etale sheafification, and then
401	to a group-scheme structure on the representing scheme by Yoneda.
402	
403	\begin{theorem}[$\Pic_{C/k}$ is a $k$-group scheme]
404	  \label{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}
405	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
406	  \uses{def:pic_scheme, thm:fga_pic_representability,
407	        def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
408	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs (the relative
409	  % Picard functor takes values in abelian groups) and \S 4 surrounding
410	  % text (group-scheme structure on $\IPic_{X/S}$ via tensor product);
411	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~17, 26 (read from
412	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318 + L2057-L2069).
413	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
414	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
415	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
416	  %      $$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
417	  %   Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
418	  %  topologies  by
419	  %      $$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
420	  %      \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
421	  %   \end{dfn}"
422	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
423	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
424	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~\ref{df:Pfs}
425	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
426	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by $\IPic_{X/S}$.  Moreover, we say simply that the
427	  %  Picard scheme $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists."
428	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs +
429	  \S 4, Def.~df:Psch (the Picard scheme).}
430	  Let $\Pic_{C/k}$ be the Picard scheme of
431	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. Then $\Pic_{C/k}$ is canonically an
432	  abelian group scheme over $k$: the tensor product
433	  $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}]$
434	  and inverse $-[\mathcal{L}] := [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$ on
435	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$ (well defined because both operations
436	  preserve isomorphism classes and descend through the subgroup
437	  $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined})
438	  are natural in $T$, hence by Yoneda are represented by morphisms
439	  $m : \Pic_{C/k} \times_k \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}$,
440	  $i : \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}$, and
441	  $e : \Spec k \to \Pic_{C/k}$ (the trivial line bundle class) satisfying
442	  the abelian-group axioms. In particular, $\Pic_{C/k}$ is a $k$-group
443	  scheme locally of finite type.
444	\end{theorem}
445	
446	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- Kleiman \S 2--4 do not isolate a
447	% separate proof block for the group-scheme structure: it is implicit in
448	% the fact that $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$ takes values in abelian groups (df:Pfs)
449	% and that representability transfers algebraic operations from set-valued
450	% functors to scheme morphisms by Yoneda. The structural restatement below
451	% records the standard transfer.
452	\begin{proof}
453	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
454	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
455	  The relative Picard functor $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ of
456	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is already abelian-group
457	  valued, with sum $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] = [\mathcal{L} \otimes
458	  \mathcal{M}]$, inverse $-[\mathcal{L}] = [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$, and unit
459	  $0 = [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]$; these operations descend through
460	  the quotient by $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ because $\pi_T^*$ is itself a
461	  group homomorphism. The \'etale sheafification step of
462	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} preserves the abelian-group structure
463	  on values, because sheafification of an abelian-presheaf is again an
464	  abelian-sheaf (the sheafification functor commutes with finite products
465	  in the value category).
466	
467	  By \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ is
468	  represented by the scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$. The Yoneda embedding is fully
469	  faithful and preserves limits, so the natural transformations
470	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \times \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to
471	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (multiplication),
472	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (inverse), and the
473	  unit map $h_{\Spec k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ pick out unique
474	  morphisms of representing schemes $m$, $i$, $e$ as displayed. The
475	  abelian-group axioms (associativity, commutativity, identity,
476	  inverse) hold at the level of $T$-points functorially in $T$, hence
477	  hold on the representing scheme by Yoneda. The local-finite-type
478	  property is part of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}.
479	\end{proof}

88	/-! ## §1. Line bundles on a product
89	
90	A *line bundle* on the relative curve `C ×_S T` is an invertible
91	`O_(C ×_S T)`-module, i.e. a sheaf of modules locally free of rank one. In
92	Mathlib `b80f227` the `Scheme.Modules` category is in place
93	(`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`), but there is no `IsInvertible`
94	predicate on its objects yet (the closest is `Module.Invertible R M` for rings,
95	in `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`). The iter-174 file-skeleton therefore
96	encodes the type of line bundles on the product by a typed `sorry` at the type
97	level; iter-175+ will instantiate it as a subtype of `(pullback πC πT).Modules`
98	cutting out invertibility.
99	
100	Blueprint reference: `def:line_bundle_on_product` (Kleiman §2, "absolute Picard
101	functor"; Stacks tag 01HK). -/
102	
103	/-- The type of **line bundles on the product** `C ×_S T`: invertible sheaves of
104	`O_(C ×_S T)`-modules, where the product is the categorical pullback of the
105	projections `πC : C ⟶ S` and `πT : T ⟶ S`. In Kleiman's notation with `X = C`
106	and base `S`, this is `Pic_C(T) = Pic(C ×_S T)`, the value of the absolute
107	Picard functor at `T`.
108	
109	iter-175+: instantiate as a subtype of `(pullback πC πT).Modules` carrying an
110	invertibility witness — concretely either
111	```
112	structure OnProduct ... where
113	  sheaf : (pullback πC πT).Modules
114	  isInvertible : IsInvertible sheaf
115	```
116	or the quotient by isomorphism for the Picard *group* refinement. For the
117	iter-174 file-skeleton this is a typed `sorry` at the type level because
118	Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `IsInvertible` predicate on `Scheme.Modules`. -/
119	noncomputable def OnProduct {S C T : Scheme.{u}} (_πC : C ⟶ S) (_πT : T ⟶ S) :
120	    Type (u+1) :=
121	  sorry
122	
123	/-! ## §2. The pullback functor along the projection
124	
125	For a morphism of schemes `f : Y ⟶ Z` and an invertible `O_Z`-module `M`, the
126	pullback `f^* M` is an invertible `O_Y`-module (the pullback of an
127	open-cover-trivialisation of `M` trivialises `f^* M`, and the pullback of a free
128	module of rank one is free of rank one — Stacks tag 01HH). Specialising to the
129	projection `π_T = pullback.snd πC πT : C ×_S T ⟶ T` gives the pullback map
130	`π_T^* : Pic(T) → Pic(C ×_S T)`. Mathlib's underlying `Scheme.Modules.pullback`
131	functor (in `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`) supplies the pullback on
132	the full category of sheaves of modules; iter-175+ will restrict it to
133	invertible sheaves once the `OnProduct` carrier is unpacked.
134	
135	Blueprint reference: `def:pullback_along_projection` (Kleiman §2; Stacks tag
136	01HH). -/
137	
138	/-- **Pullback along the projection** `π_T : C ×_S T ⟶ T` of a relative
139	curve. Sends an `O_T`-line bundle `N` to the `O_(C ×_S T)`-line bundle
140	`π_T^* N`, induced by the underlying `Scheme.Modules.pullback` functor on
141	`Scheme.Modules`.
142	
143	iter-175+: instantiate as `(Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N`
144	wrapped with the carried `IsInvertible` witness (the pullback of an invertible

148	`T.Modules → OnProduct πC πT`, which after unfolding `OnProduct` becomes the

150	noncomputable def pullbackAlongProjection {S C T : Scheme.{u}}
151	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (_N : T.Modules) : OnProduct πC πT :=

154	/-! ## §3. Composition of pullbacks
155	
156	The composition law for line-bundle pullback (Stacks tag 01HG) says that for any
157	composable morphisms `f : X ⟶ Y, g : Y ⟶ Z` and any line bundle `M ∈ Pic(Z)`,
158	the natural isomorphism `(g ∘ f)^* M ≅ f^* g^* M` makes the pullback functor
159	into a (pseudo-)functor `Sch^op ⥤ Cat`.
160	
161	Specialised to the relative curve setting: given `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`, the
162	base-change morphism `g_C := id_C ×_S g : C ×_S T' ⟶ C ×_S T` satisfies
163	`π_T ∘ g_C = g ∘ π_{T'}` (definition of the fiber product). Therefore on
164	line bundles `g_C^* ∘ π_T^* = π_{T'}^* ∘ g^*`, with the equality realised as the
165	canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors via the pseudo-functoriality
166	of `Scheme.Modules.pullback` (Mathlib `Modules.pullbackComp`).
167	
168	Blueprint reference: `lem:pullback_compose` (Kleiman §2; Stacks tag 01HG). -/
169	
170	/-- **Composition of line-bundle pullbacks** along the projection. For a base
171	scheme `S`, a curve-side morphism `πC : C ⟶ S`, test objects `T, T'` over `S`
172	via `πT : T ⟶ S` and `πT' : T' ⟶ S`, and a morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`
173	(encoded by the hypothesis `πT' = g ≫ πT`), set
174	`g_C := id_C ×_S g : C ×_S T' ⟶ C ×_S T`
175	(the base-change morphism, given by `pullback.map`). On the underlying
176	`Scheme.Modules.pullback` functors the two routes through the canonical
177	naturality square give canonically isomorphic objects:
178	```
179	            Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)
180	  T.Modules  ─────────────────────────────────────────────►  (C ×_S T).Modules
181	       │                                                              │
182	       │ Scheme.Modules.pullback g            Scheme.Modules.pullback g_C
183	       ▼                                                              ▼
184	 T'.Modules  ─────────────────────────────────────────────►  (C ×_S T').Modules
185	            Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT')
186	```
187	i.e. for any `N : T.Modules`,
188	```
189	(Modules.pullback g_C).obj ((Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N)
190	  ≅
191	(Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT')).obj ((Modules.pullback g).obj N).
192	```
193	
194	This natural isomorphism is the substantive content of the
195	"line-bundle pullback functor is well-behaved under composition" fact
196	(Stacks tag 01HG). At the level of the `OnProduct` carrier (which is a typed
197	`sorry` in iter-174; see §1) it descends to equality of isomorphism classes
198	`[g_C^* π_T^* N] = [π_{T'}^* g^* N] ∈ OnProduct πC πT'`.
199	
200	iter-175+: the body is `Modules.pullbackComp` composed twice (once on each
201	route) and patched together by the equation `πT' = g ≫ πT` (which gives
202	`π_T ∘ g_C = g ∘ π_{T'}` from the pullback square). For the iter-174
203	file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
204	theorem pullback_pullback_eq {S C T T' : Scheme.{u}}
205	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (πT' : T' ⟶ S) (g : T' ⟶ T)
206	    (hg : πT' = g ≫ πT) (N : T.Modules) :
207	    Nonempty
208	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback
209	            (Limits.pullback.map πC πT' πC πT (𝟙 C) g (𝟙 S)
210	              (by rw [Category.comp_id, Category.id_comp]) (by rw [Category.comp_id, hg]))).obj
211	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N) ≅
212	        (Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT')).obj
213	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g).obj N)) := by
214	  sorry
215	
216	end LineBundle

220	/-! ## §4. The relative Picard quotient (set-valued)
221	
222	The relative Picard presheaf `Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) := Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)`
223	quotients the line-bundle group of the product by the subgroup pulled back from
224	the base. As a quotient of abelian groups it is a quotient set; equivalently,
225	the equivalence relation `L ~ L' ↔ L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)` is captured by a
226	`Setoid` on the line-bundle type `OnProduct πC πT`. The quotient
227	`Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT)` is the underlying set of the relative
228	Picard presheaf at `T`.
229	
230	The "preimage_subgroup" name follows the Kleiman/Stacks convention: the
231	quotient is by the preimage `π_T^* Pic(T) ⊆ Pic(C ×_S T)`, treated as a
232	subgroup (image of a group homomorphism); the project-side Lean encoding
233	extracts the *Setoid* that the quotient construction takes.
234	
235	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined` (Kleiman §2,
236	Def. `df:Pfs`). -/
237	
238	/-- **Well-definedness of the relative Picard quotient as a `Setoid`.**
239	
240	The equivalence relation on `OnProduct πC πT` defining the relative Picard
241	presheaf
242	```
243	Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) := Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)
244	```
245	is the relation `L ~ L' ↔ L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)`. This relation is reflexive
246	(`L ⊗ L⁻¹ = O_(C ×_S T) = π_T^* O_T ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)`), symmetric (the inverse of
247	a line bundle in `π_T^* Pic(T)` is in `π_T^* Pic(T)`), and transitive
248	(`L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)` and `L' ⊗ L''⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)` give
249	`L ⊗ L''⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)`); hence is an equivalence relation, encoded by a
250	`Setoid` on `OnProduct πC πT`.
251	
252	The substantive content is the well-definedness of the quotient: the canonical

# Auslander–Buchsbaum formula (A.4.b)

This file is the **A.4.b** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the
Auslander–Buchsbaum formula and the corollary "regular local ring
⟹ Cohen–Macaulay" that the sibling sub-build **A.4.a** (codim-1 extension of a
rational map across a codim-2 closed point on a regular projective surface)
consumes.

Per STRATEGY.md L30 this row is gated downstream on A.4.a but is independently
startable on the Mathlib side: the algebra here is decoupled from the Albanese
geometry and lives entirely under `RingTheory.*`. Mathlib at the project's
pinned commit (`b80f227`) exposes `IsRegularLocalRing` and the categorical
`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` on an abelian category (specialised to
`ModuleCat R`), as well as the regular-sequence definition
`RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular`. The depth function `Module.depth`, the
Auslander–Buchsbaum formula, and the Cohen–Macaulay predicate are *not* in
Mathlib at the pinned commit — they are the new content scaffolded here.

## Status (iter-175 Lane F file-skeleton)

This file is the **iter-175 Lane F** file-skeleton: each of the seven
blueprint-pinned declarations carries the *intended* substantive type
signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
`blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum.tex`) with a `sorry` body.
The bodies are iter-176+ work; the chapter is independent of the rest of
Route A's geometric infrastructure, making it a good parallel-work candidate.

The 7 pinned declarations are:

1. `RingTheory.Module.depth` (noncomputable def, ~6 LOC) — the `I`-depth of a
   finite `R`-module as the supremum of lengths of `M`-regular sequences in
   `I`, valued in `ℕ∞`.
2. `RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — for
   `(R, 𝔪)` Noetherian local and `M ≠ 0` finite, `depth(M)` equals the
   smallest `i` with `Ext^i_R(κ, M) ≠ 0`. Encoded via the depth-bound iff
   `Ext` vanishes below.
3. `Module.projectiveDimension` (noncomputable def, ~3 LOC) — an
   `R`-module-side wrapper for the categorical
   `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R M)`. The categorical
   version exists in Mathlib `b80f227`; this is the re-export pinned by the
   blueprint.
4. `RingTheory.depth_of_short_exact` (theorem, ~12 LOC) — the
   depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequalities (Stacks 00LE).
5. `RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the formula
   `pd_R(M) + depth(M) = depth(R)` for a nonzero finite `R`-module of finite
   projective dimension over a Noetherian local ring.
6. `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay` (class, ~3 LOC) — `IsCohenMacaulayLocalRing R`

7. `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular` (theorem, ~6 LOC) — a regular local

## Note on type expressivity

Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:

- `depth` returns `ℕ∞` and is defined by the regular-sequence supremum.
- `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` is encoded as the depth-bound `↔` `Ext`

/-! ## §3. Depth via Ext characterisation

For a Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` with residue field `κ = R/𝔪` and a
nonzero finite `R`-module `M`, the depth of `M` equals the smallest index `i`
at which `Ext^i_R(κ, M)` is nonzero (Stacks tag 00LP).

This is the lemma A.4.a's downstream consumer ultimately reads off:
combined with the regular-sequence definition (`depth`), the Ext
characterisation provides both the *lower bound* (regular sequences exhibit
`Ext^i = 0` for `i < length(rs)`) and the *upper bound* (failure of any
extension lifts a non-zero element in `Ext^{depth}(κ, M)`).

The signature pins the equivalence via the depth-bound `↔` `Ext`-vanishing-
below: `n ≤ depth(M) ↔ Ext^i(κ, M) = 0 for all i < n`. This is logically
equivalent to "depth(M) = smallest i with Ext^i ≠ 0" and is the form most
convenient for inductive proofs (Stacks 00LP proof: pick `x ∈ 𝔪`
non-zero-divisor, use long exact `Ext^*(κ, -)` on `0 → M → M → M/xM → 0`).

Blueprint reference: `lem:depth_via_ext` (Stacks tag 00LP). -/

/-- **Depth via Ext characterisation.** For a Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)`

/-! ## §4. Depth on a short exact sequence

For a short exact sequence `0 → N' → N → N'' → 0` of nonzero finite modules
over a Noetherian local ring, the three modules' depths satisfy three
crosswise inequalities (Stacks tag 00LE), each a direct read-off of the
long exact `Ext^*(κ, -)` sequence and the depth-via-Ext characterisation

Blueprint reference: `lem:depth_short_exact_sequence` (Stacks tag 00LE). -/

/-- **Depth on a short exact sequence.** Let `(R, 𝔪)` be a Noetherian local
ring and let
```
0 → N' → N → N'' → 0
```
be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite `R`-modules (encoded by an
injection `f : N' →ₗ[R] N`, a surjection `g : N →ₗ[R] N''`, and
`Function.Exact f g`). Then
1. `depth(N) ≥ min(depth(N'), depth(N''))`,
2. `depth(N'') ≥ min(depth(N), depth(N') - 1)`,
3. `depth(N') ≥ min(depth(N), depth(N'') + 1)`.

The three inequalities are read off from the long exact `Ext^*(κ, -)`
sequence applied to the short exact sequence and the depth-via-Ext
characterisation (§3 above).

    [AddCommGroup N'] [Module R N'] [_root_.Module.Finite R N'] [Nontrivial N']
    [AddCommGroup N] [Module R N] [_root_.Module.Finite R N] [Nontrivial N]
    [AddCommGroup N''] [Module R N''] [_root_.Module.Finite R N''] [Nontrivial N'']
    (f : N' →ₗ[R] N) (g : N →ₗ[R] N'')

    (_hex : Function.Exact f g) :
    min (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N')
        (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N'')
      ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N
    ∧ min (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N)
          (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N' - 1)
        ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N''
    ∧ min (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N)
          (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N'' + 1)
        ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N' := by
  sorry

/-! ## §5. The Auslander–Buchsbaum formula

For a nonzero finite module `M` of finite projective dimension over a
Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)`, the **Auslander–Buchsbaum formula** reads
```
  pd_R(M) + depth(M) = depth(R)
```
(Stacks tag 090V). The proof inducts on `depth(M)`: the base case
`depth(M) = 0` uses a minimal finite free resolution of `M` and the
"what is exact" criterion (Stacks 00MF) plus iterated application of the
depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma (§4) to bound `depth(R)` against the
resolution length; the inductive step picks a common non-zero-divisor
`x ∈ 𝔪` on both `R` and `M`, applies the snake lemma to obtain a minimal
finite free resolution of `M/xM` over `R/xR` of the same length, and uses
the inductive hypothesis on `M/xM` over `R/xR`.

Blueprint reference: `thm:auslander_buchsbaum` (Stacks tag 090V). -/

/-- **The Auslander–Buchsbaum formula.** Let `(R, 𝔪)` be a Noetherian local
ring and let `M` be a nonzero finite `R`-module of finite projective
dimension. Then
```
  pd_R(M) + depth_R(M) = depth(R).
```

The hypothesis "finite projective dimension" is encoded by an explicit
upper bound `n : ℕ` on the projective dimension (so the formula compares
finite numeric quantities cleanly without `WithBot ℕ∞`-arithmetic
subtleties).

"what is exact" + iterated `Module.depth_of_short_exact`; inductive step
via the snake lemma on multiplication by a common non-zero-divisor and
the inductive hypothesis on `M/xM` over `R/xR`. For the iter-175
file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
theorem auslander_buchsbaum_formula
    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
    {M : Type u} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [_root_.Module.Finite R M]
    [Nontrivial M]
    (n : ℕ)
    (_hpd : _root_.Module.projectiveDimension R M = (n : WithBot ℕ∞)) :
    (n : ℕ∞) + Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M
      = Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R := by
  sorry

/-! ## §6. Cohen–Macaulay local rings

A Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` is **Cohen–Macaulay** if its depth equals
its Krull dimension (Stacks tag 00N4). Mathlib `b80f227` has neither the
predicate nor the class — this file is the upstream gap-fill.

Blueprint reference: `def:cohen_macaulay_local` (Stacks tag 00N4). -/

/-- A Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` is **Cohen–Macaulay** if its depth
equals its Krull dimension: `depth(R) = dim R`.

Encoded as a `Prop`-valued type class so downstream consumers can write
`[CohenMacaulay R]` and use Cohen–Macaulay as a hypothesis. Mathlib at the
pinned commit (`b80f227`) does not expose any Cohen–Macaulay predicate;
this is the upstream gap-fill.

iter-176+: the predicate is `Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R =
ringKrullDim R`. For the iter-175 file-skeleton the carrier definition is a
typed `sorry` at the `Prop` level — substantively, the predicate is the
named equality, but we package it as a `class` so use sites are uniform. -/
class CohenMacaulay (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
    [IsNoetherianRing R] : Prop where
  /-- The Cohen–Macaulay equation: `depth(R) = ringKrullDim R`. The numeric
  comparison is in `WithBot ℕ∞` after coercion of the `ℕ∞`-valued depth. -/
  depth_eq_krullDim :
    (Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R : WithBot ℕ∞) = ringKrullDim R

/-! ## §7. Regular local rings are Cohen–Macaulay

The consumer-facing input for A.4.a: every regular Noetherian local ring is
Cohen–Macaulay (Stacks tag 00OD). The direct proof: pick a minimal
generating set `x_1, …, x_d` of `𝔪` (where `d = dim R`), use that `R` is a
domain (Stacks 00NQ) to start an `R`-regular sequence, and induct on
dimension — each `R/(x_1, …, x_c)` is again regular of dimension `d - c`,
so `x_1, …, x_d` is an `R`-regular sequence and `depth(R) ≥ d`. The reverse
inequality `depth(R) ≤ dim R` is the standard depth bound (Stacks 00LK).

Blueprint reference: `cor:regular_cohen_macaulay` (Stacks tag 00OD). -/

namespace CohenMacaulay

/-- **Regular local rings are Cohen–Macaulay.** Every regular Noetherian
local ring is Cohen–Macaulay: a minimal generating set of `𝔪` is an
`R`-regular sequence of length `dim R`, so `depth(R) ≥ dim R`; combined
with the standard upper bound `depth(R) ≤ dim R` (Stacks 00LK) this gives
`depth(R) = dim R`.

This is the consumer-facing input for **A.4.a** (codim-1 extension of a
rational map across a codim-2 closed point on a regular projective
surface): the local ring `O_{S,x}` of a regular projective surface at a
closed point is regular of Krull dimension `2`, hence Cohen–Macaulay,
hence has depth `2`, which is exactly the input the local-cohomology
vanishing `H^i_x(O_S) = 0` for `i < 2` needs (Stacks 0AVF; see Hartshorne
III.7).

iter-176+: the body is either the direct regular-sequence argument
outlined in the chapter, or via Auslander–Buchsbaum applied to all finite
`R`-modules (any finite `R`-module has `pd_R(M) ≤ d`; specialising to
suitable `M` gives the equation). For the iter-175 file-skeleton the body
is a typed `sorry`. -/
instance of_regular (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
    [IsNoetherianRing R] [IsRegularLocalRing R] : CohenMacaulay R where
  depth_eq_krullDim := by
    sorry

end CohenMacaulay

end RingTheory

import Mathlib

/-!
# Milne Theorem 3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian
variety extends (A.4.c)

This file is the **A.4.c** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the single
theorem of Milne's *Abelian Varieties* §I.3, Theorem 3.2:
**a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends,
uniquely, to a regular morphism**. This is the input that the Albanese
universal-property build (A.4.d, `Albanese/AlbaneseUP.lean`) consumes when
promoting the symmetric-product rational map
`C^{(g)} ⇢ A` to a regular morphism `J → A`.

Milne's proof is two lines: combine Theorem 3.1 (the codim-$\geq 2$ extension
result for rational maps from a normal variety to a complete variety —
project-side `thm:codim_one_extension` of
`Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean`, A.4.a) with Lemma 3.3 (the
pure-codim-$1$ indeterminacy structure for rational maps into a group variety
— project-side `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy` of the same chapter). The
combination forces the indeterminacy locus of `f` to be simultaneously of
codimension `≥ 2` and pure codimension `1`, hence empty, so `f` is everywhere
defined.

## Status (iter-175 Lane J file-skeleton)

This file is the **iter-175 Lane J** file-skeleton: the single pinned
declaration carries the intended substantive type signature (matching the
blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in
`chapters/Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex` line 50) with a `sorry`
body. The body is iter-176+ work, gated on the sibling chapters
`Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean` (A.4.a) and
`Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean` (A.4.b) landing their substantive
statements.

The 1 pinned declaration is:

1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av` (theorem, ~10 LOC)
   — Milne Theorem 3.2: for `X` a smooth (= nonsingular) variety over an
   algebraically closed field `k̄`, `A` an abelian variety over `k̄`, and
   `f : X ⇢ A` a rational map, there exists a *unique* regular morphism
   `g : X ⟶ A` whose induced rational map equals `f`.

## Note on type expressivity

The signature is encoded against Mathlib's existing
`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap` (a partial-map equivalence class on
dense opens) and `Scheme.Hom.toRationalMap` (the canonical "regular morphism
yields a rational map" coercion). The substantive claim is the existence
and uniqueness of a `g : X.left ⟶ A.left` with `g.toRationalMap = f`. The
type is non-tautological: the existence is the content of Milne 3.2;
uniqueness is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement principle
(`AlgebraicGeometry.ext_of_isDominant`).

## Abelian-variety conventions

Throughout the project (cf. `AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean`, `Jacobian.lean`),
an **abelian variety over `k̄`** is encoded as an object
`A : Over (Spec (.of k̄))` carrying the four instances:

- `[GrpObj A]` (group-object structure on the over-category),
- `[IsProper A.hom]` (complete),
- `[Smooth A.hom]` (nonsingular),
- `[GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]` (geometrically irreducible).

A **nonsingular variety over `k̄`** (in the convention of
`blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex` §1) is an
object `X : Over (Spec (.of k̄))` carrying:

- `[Smooth X.hom]`,
- `[GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]`,
- `[IsSeparated X.hom]`,
- `[LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]`,
- `[IsIntegral X.left]`,
- `[IsReduced X.left]`.

These four-plus-two instances will normally be supplied by `inferInstance` at
the call site.

## References

Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex`
(250 LOC, 1 pin). Source: Milne, *Abelian Varieties*, §I.3, Theorem 3.2,
p. 17 (`references/abelian-varieties.pdf`, PDF page 23).
-/

This is the input the Albanese universal-property build
(`Albanese/AlbaneseUP.lean`, A.4.d) consumes when promoting the
symmetric-product rational map `C^{(g)} ⇢ A` to a regular morphism
`J → A`.

iter-176+: body combines `thm:codim_one_extension` (A.4.a; codim-$\geq 2$
extension to a normal variety target — here the abelian variety, which is
complete) with `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy` (A.4.a; pure-codim-$1$
indeterminacy for maps into a group variety). The two combine to force the
indeterminacy locus empty; uniqueness is the standard agreement principle
for reduced `X` and separated `A`. -/
theorem extend_to_av
    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
    {X : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
    [Smooth X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
    [IsSeparated X.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]
    [IsIntegral X.left] [IsReduced X.left]
    {A : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
    [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom] [Smooth A.hom]
    [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]
    (f : X.left.RationalMap A.left) :
    ∃! (g : X.left ⟶ A.left), g.toRationalMap = f := by
  sorry

end RationalMap

end Scheme

end AlgebraicGeometry

namespace Combinator.Calculus
  def I (a : α) : α := a
  def K (a : α) : β → α := fun _ => a
  def S (x : α → β → γ) (y : α → β) (z : α) : γ := x z (y z)
end Combinator.Calculus

section
  -- open everything under `Combinator.Calculus`, *i.e.* `I`, `K` and `S`,
  -- until the section ends
  open Combinator.Calculus

  theorem SKx_eq_K : S K x = I := rfl
end

-- open everything under `Combinator.Calculus` only for the next command (the next `theorem`, here)
open Combinator.Calculus in
theorem SKx_eq_K' : S K x = I := rfl

section
  -- open only `S` and `K` under `Combinator.Calculus`
  open Combinator.Calculus (S K)

  theorem SKxy_eq_y : S K x y = y := rfl

  -- `I` is not in scope, we have to use its full path
  theorem SKxy_eq_Iy : S K x y = Combinator.Calculus.I y := rfl
end

  #check konstant
